VitestRules.keywords: vitest, runner, assertions, ai_first_tests, contract_testing, unit_testing, mocks, vi_mock, factories, unified_context, file_size_budget, snapshots, bdd_mapping, case_flow, isolation, fake_timers, dependency_injection
VitestRules.Mission:
Canonical rules for writing tests on the Vitest runner (`vitest`) for every execution-agent in the project.
Goal: contract-focused, isolated, deterministic, semantically annotated suite that another AI agent can read, trust, run, and extend without reverse-engineering.
Vitest-flavored sibling of `testing/node-test`: shared testing philosophy is identical and inherited by reference (see `Inherited_Baseline`). What differs and is stated in full here: Vitest API, mocking surface, lifecycle vocabulary, unified-context discipline, file-size budget. Domain-specific testing (UI components, e2e Playwright, browser-mode Vitest, integration with infra) NOT covered here.
Tests are CODE: they obey the same machine-first annotation language as production code (`coding/typescript-rules`) and the shared testing axioms of `testing/node-test`.
VitestRules.Inherited_Baseline:
The following axioms from `testing/node-test` apply UNCHANGED here and are NOT restated. Reader-agent treats their wording as binding under this directive. Where a Vitest-specific override exists, it is named explicitly in `Belief_State` below.
`AX_CONTRACT_OVER_IMPLEMENTATION`, `AX_ISOLATION_WITH_BEHAVIORAL_EVIDENCE`, `AX_EVENT_CONTRACT_BOUNDARY`, `AX_CANCELLATION_CONTRACT_BOUNDARY`, `AX_TESTS_AS_EXECUTABLE_LEARNING`, `AX_UNIFIED_ANNOTATION_LANGUAGE`, `AX_CASE_FLOW_OVER_RIGID_AAA`, `AX_PHASE_PRESENCE_RULES`, `AX_PHASE_ANCHORS_MANDATORY`, `AX_CASE_INNER_ANCHORS`, `AX_OBSERVE_AS_AGGREGATION_PLACEMENT`, `AX_NO_FAKE_AAA_SPLIT`, `AX_IT_OPENING_LEARNING_BRIEF`, `AX_IT_TRIVIAL_SKIP_BRIEF`, `AX_DIAGNOSTIC_SHAPE_FIRST`, `AX_COLLAPSE_ONLY_WHEN_CLEARER`, `AX_DEFAULT_DIRECT_ASSERTION_PROTOCOL`, `AX_PARTIAL_STRING_VIA_MATCH`, `AX_RESULT_UNWRAP_ON_SUCCESS_PATHS`, `AX_FOCUSED_ERROR_ASSERTIONS`, `AX_NO_FRAGMENTATION_OF_COHERENT_CONTRACT`, `AX_FIXTURE_IO_POLICY`, `AX_COVERAGE_BY_CONTRACT_NOT_BY_LINE`, `AX_COVERAGE_REPORT_BLOCKER_EXPLICIT`, `AX_FILE_NAME_AND_DEFAULT_SHAPE`, `AX_TEST_ANCHOR_NAMING`, `AX_TEST_ANCHOR_INNER_RULE`, `AX_TEST_ANCHOR_TRIVIAL_EXCEPTION`, `AX_TEST_ANCHOR_COMMENT_PAIRING`, `AX_ONE_TEST_ONE_SCENARIO`, `AX_TESTS_NO_HUMAN_NARRATION`, `AX_FOCUS_ON_OBSERVABLE_BEHAVIOR`, `AX_ENGLISH_ONLY_NAMES_AND_COMMENTS`, `AX_VERIFY_AND_FINALIZE`.
The full `BDD_Mapping_Hint` block of `testing/node-test` applies here verbatim, with these mechanical substitutions: `assert.throws()` → `expect(fn).toThrow(...)`; `assert.rejects()` → `await expect(promise).rejects.toThrow(...)`; `assert.match()` → `expect(str).toMatch(regex)`.

Belief_State.Axiom:
**Primary `test` script MUST invoke `vitest run` (one-shot), never bare `vitest` (defaults to watch in TTY).**
**✅ Required:** ```json "test": "vitest run", // ✅ one-shot, exits 0/1 "test:watch": "vitest", // ✅ explicit watch (human dev) "test:coverage": "vitest run --coverage" // ✅ one-shot with coverage ```
**❌ Forbidden:** ```json "test": "vitest" // ❌ defaults to watch in TTY → agent hangs "test": "vitest --watch" // ❌ explicit watch ```
AI agents and CI runners invoke `npm test` and wait for exit. Bare `vitest` in TTY enters interactive watch mode, streams forever, never exits. Agent's session hangs indefinitely with no way to distinguish «still running» from «ready, waiting for changes». Top cause of stuck infrastructure-setup tickets — see `nodejs-npm-rules.xml#AX_AI_FIRST_SCRIPTS_ONE_SHOT`.
Vitest specifics: `vitest` autodetects TTY and switches to watch. `vitest run` forces one-shot regardless of TTY. There is no «default no-watch» config flag — discipline at script-definition level. Interactive human dev: `npm run test:watch` explicitly.

**Each test file MUST define exactly ONE context shape** — a single `XxxContext` type and a single factory `createXxxContext(overrides?)` (or, when teardown required, a single lifecycle context object built in `beforeEach` and disposed in `afterEach`).
Every new case in the file consumes this same context. New requirements grow the existing context shape and factory through optional fields and overrides; they do NOT spawn parallel `createAnotherThingContext` factories or sibling `let`s in the describe.
Forbidden: a file with `createOrderContextA` + `createOrderContextB` + ad-hoc inline construction. Allowed and required: one `createOrderContext(overrides)` enriched as scenarios accumulate.
A nested `describe` MAY introduce its own narrower context only when its sub-group has lifecycle requirements that cannot be expressed as overrides on the parent context; this exception must be obvious from the diff.
Why: test files stay small and predictable — one shape applies to every case. New tests almost always add an override, not new structure → caps marginal cost per case.

Default test data preparation = the file's single factory called from inside the case. Each case constructs the exact context it needs by calling `createXxxContext({ ... overrides })` at the top of the case, destructures only the fields it uses, and proceeds. `beforeEach` is reserved for teardown-bound setup (per `AX_HOOKS_ONLY_FOR_LIFECYCLE`).
Per-case dependencies become visible at the call site; no scrolling to a hook. Diffs shrink: changing one case does not edit a shared hook and ripple-impact unrelated cases. No hidden temporal couplings; no shared mutable `let` populated in `beforeEach`.

`beforeEach` / `afterEach` are reserved for context that REQUIRES teardown — open resources (timers, sockets, file handles, DB connections), global stubs (`vi.stubGlobal`, `vi.stubEnv`), module mocks, fake timers, fixture filesystems, in-process servers.
When a hook is justified, it MUST follow the **single-context shape**: hook builds ONE context object and `afterEach` disposes it. Tests consume properties of that single context.
Forbidden: a `describe` block declaring multiple `let`-variables and a `beforeEach` that assigns them one by one. A pile of `let` + diffuse assignments in `beforeEach` hides what each test actually depends on.

Within a single `describe` choose ONE preparation path: factory-only OR lifecycle-context-only. Do NOT combine «factory for some fields + `beforeEach` for others» inside the same describe — that splits the state model. A consistent path inside a describe lets the reader-agent answer «where does this value come from?» by inspection, not by tracing.

`describe`-scope MUST NOT host mutable `let` variables populated by hooks. Static fixtures (frozen constants, fixture paths, factory definitions, type aliases) are allowed. Anything mutable per-case lives either inside the case (factory result) or inside the SINGLE lifecycle context object exposed by the hook. Mutable describe-scope state is the structural source of inter-test leakage and makes parallel/random execution unsafe.

**Soft target: ≤300 lines per test file. Hard ceiling: 500 lines.** Counted lines = code only (blank lines and comments excluded). At 300 → look for natural split points; at 500 → split mandatory.
Split axes, in priority order: 1. By SUT method (one method = one `[subject].[method].test.ts` file, sharing the parent-level factory via a small shared helper module if context is reused). 2. By scenario family (e.g., `[subject].error-paths.test.ts` separate from `[subject].happy-paths.test.ts`) — only when method axis does not yield a clean split.
Unified context (`AX_ONE_UNIFIED_CONTEXT_PER_FILE`) survives a split: factory moves to a sibling `[subject].test-context.ts` and is imported by each test file. After the split each file STILL holds exactly one context shape and reuses the same factory.
Why: instruction-bearing files (CLAUDE.md guidance) target <300 lines because instruction-following degrades with size. Cross-vendor AI-coding sweet spot 150–500 LOC driven by token cost per edit and agent's ability to re-read the whole file. A test file IS instruction-bearing (teaches the next agent what the contract is). Past 500 LOC the agent loses the file's structure between read/edit cycles → fabricated green tests slip in.

**Prefer real modules with dependency injection over `vi.mock` / `vi.spyOn` / `vi.hoisted`.**
A SUT that takes its collaborators as constructor or function arguments is tested by passing test doubles directly through those seams — no module-level mocking needed.
Module-level mocking allowed ONLY when ALL of: 1. Dependency is genuinely external (network, FS, process, OS time, randomness, third-party SDKs touching the network). 2. No available injection seam in the SUT (refactoring to add one is out of scope). 3. Case carries an explicit justification comment naming the dependency and the reason injection is unavailable.
Mocks of pure in-process neighboring modules are forbidden by default. Every mock freezes an assumption about another module's behavior; injection keeps the seam observable in the type system.

Introducing a NEW `vi.mock(...)` or `vi.hoisted(...)` call in a test file is a gated action. Execution-agent MUST stop before introducing such a mock and request explicit operator confirmation in chat, presenting: - module being mocked, - why dependency injection cannot be used here, - what observable contract the mock preserves.
Re-using an existing project-level mock helper does NOT require a new confirmation. `vi.mock` is the single biggest lever for fabricating green tests; confirm-gate makes that fabrication visible.

A test MUST NOT mock the very module that contains the SUT, its direct collaborator under test, or the contract method being verified. Mocking the SUT or its co-tested collaborator turns the test into a tautology: it asserts the mock instead of production code. Most common form of fabricated green tests in agent-written suites. Non-negotiable.

When mocks are used, lifecycle hygiene is mandatory. Either set `clearMocks: true` (and `unstubEnvs: true`, `unstubGlobals: true` when stubs used) in `vitest.config`, OR call `vi.clearAllMocks()` in `afterEach`.
Reset granularity: - `vi.clearAllMocks()` — clear call history; keep implementations. - `vi.resetAllMocks()` — clear history AND reset implementations to empty `vi.fn()`. - `vi.restoreAllMocks()` — restore originals of `vi.spyOn`-created spies. - `vi.unstubAllEnvs()` / `vi.unstubAllGlobals()` — pair every `stubEnv`/`stubGlobal` with these in `afterEach`.
Without disciplined reset, mock state leaks across cases and creates order-dependent flicker.

For tracking call interactions on injected collaborators, use `vi.fn()` directly. `vi.spyOn(target, 'method')` allowed when SUT receives a real object (not an injection seam) and a specific method must be observed without replacing the rest. Even then prefer refactoring to an injection seam. `vi.fn()` on an injected port is the cleanest interaction surface.

Default way to test HTTP-touching code is to inject the HTTP client (or a typed `request` port) into the SUT and stub it with `vi.fn()`.
Direct `globalThis.fetch` replacement, `vi.mock('node:http')`, or full module mocks of HTTP libraries are last-resort and gated by `AX_MOCK_REQUIRES_OPERATOR_CONFIRM`.
If the project already standardizes on a network-mocking harness (e.g., MSW, in-house mock-server helper), reuse it consistently; do NOT introduce a parallel mocking path. Choosing a network-mocking library is an architecture decision, not a per-test decision.
Injection puts the network boundary in the type system. A stub on the injected port is local to the case and cannot leak; replacing globals is global by definition.

State-mutating Vitest helpers MUST be paired with their teardown in `afterEach` (or via `vitest.config` flags): - `vi.useFakeTimers()` ↔ `vi.useRealTimers()` (use `vi.setSystemTime(date)` for clock). - `vi.stubEnv(...)` ↔ `vi.unstubAllEnvs()` (or `unstubEnvs: true`). - `vi.stubGlobal(...)` ↔ `vi.unstubAllGlobals()` (or `unstubGlobals: true`).
Forbidden: enabling any of these in a single case without restoring — leaks into every later case in the same file. Leaked timers/env/globals are a classic source of order-dependent flicker that looks random.

Pick the simplest direct match by shape (Vitest API): - `expect(x).toBe(y)` — scalars and reference identity (NOT for structural equality). - `expect(x).toEqual(y)` — deep structural equality, ignores `undefined` properties on the actual side. - `expect(x).toStrictEqual(y)` — deep equality including `undefined`, prototype, sparse arrays. Prefer over `toEqual` when contract distinguishes «absent» from `undefined`. - `expect(fn).toThrow(...)` / `await expect(promise).rejects.toThrow(...)` — thrown failures. - `expect(str).toMatch(regex)` — partial string / error-message checks. - `expect(x).toMatchInlineSnapshot(...)` — only under `AX_SNAPSHOT_USAGE_GATE`.
Forbidden: `toContain` on `error.message` (use `toMatch`); `toBeTruthy` / `toBeFalsy` to fold a structured assertion into a boolean (use the structured form); `try/catch + expect.fail` instead of `rejects.toThrow`.

Snapshots ONLY for stable serializable outputs that are too large to assert inline as a literal (generated text, HTML, JSON documents, codegen results). Do NOT replace scalar or small-object equality with a snapshot.
Prefer `toMatchInlineSnapshot()` over external `.snap` files for small-to-medium outputs so the contract shape stays visible directly in the test source. External `.snap` files acceptable only for outputs too large to embed inline without harming readability.
A snapshot hides the contract under a «diff blob»; for small equalities it degrades diagnostics. Inline snapshots keep the contract visible in code review.

**Execution-agent MUST NOT update snapshots autonomously.**
Forbidden: running `vitest --update` / `vitest -u`; calling `expect.addSnapshotSerializer` to silently re-shape diffs; deleting `.snap` files; editing inline-snapshot literals to make a test pass.
Allowed: writing the FIRST snapshot for a NEW test (operator reviews in PR/diff). For any change to an existing snapshot the agent MUST stop, present old vs new content, and wait for explicit operator confirmation.
External `.snap` files live in `__snapshots__/` next to the test (Vitest default). Auto-updating is the most direct path an agent has to fabricate a green test.

Import every Vitest API explicitly from `vitest` (`describe`, `it`, `expect`, `vi`, `beforeEach`, `afterEach`, `beforeAll`, `afterAll`). Do NOT rely on `globals: true` in `vitest.config`. Explicit imports make the file self-describing for humans, TypeScript, ESLint, and agent grep. Globals create invisible config dependencies.

Vitest's default `isolate: true` is preserved. Do NOT weaken isolation in `vitest.config` without an architectural justification recorded in the project. Disabling isolation trades determinism for speed and re-introduces inter-test leakage.

`it.only` / `describe.only` / `it.skip` / `describe.skip` / `it.todo` MUST NOT be present in committed code unless paired with an inline reference to a tracked deferred-ownership task. A focused `.only` silently disables the rest of the suite; a stray `.skip` rots into a coverage hole.

Definitions.Definition: The single per-file `XxxContext` type plus its single factory `createXxxContext(overrides?)` (or the single `beforeEach`/`afterEach`-managed lifecycle object). Every case in the file uses this one shape. New scenarios extend it via optional fields and overrides rather than introducing parallel structures.

A constructor parameter, function argument, or factory option of the SUT through which a collaborator can be supplied from outside. Tests use injection seams to pass `vi.fn()` stubs without resorting to module-level mocks.

Inherited from `testing/node-test` `DEF_NON_TRIVIAL_CASE`: async with setup, retries/fallbacks/errors, emitted events / external side effects, more than one meaningful observation, or nested control flow that another agent could misread at a glance.
Inherited: `// #region START_[CASE]_[PHASE]_[INTENT]` and paired `// #endregion END_[CASE]_[PHASE]_[INTENT]`. PHASE ∈ {`SETUP`, `TRIGGER`, `OBSERVE`, `ASSERT`, `CLEANUP`}.

Pattern.Intent: Default shape: ONE context type + ONE factory for the whole file. Two cases below differ only by factory overrides, not by structure. Adding a third case → ONE more override or extending the existing factory with one optional field — never a parallel factory.
Pattern.Why: One `OrderLifecycleContext` type, one `createOrderLifecycleContext` factory; failure case overrides exactly one field and reuses everything else (`AX_ONE_UNIFIED_CONTEXT_PER_FILE`, `AX_PREFER_FACTORY_OVER_HOOKS`). No describe-scope `let`s; stubs flow through injection seams; no module-level mocks.

Pattern.Intent: Justified hook usage: SUT requires teardown-bound setup (fake timers + env stub). A SINGLE lifecycle context object is built in `beforeEach` and disposed in `afterEach`; tests consume fields off that context. No `let`-pileup.
Pattern.Why: Fake timers and env stubs are teardown-bound — cannot be cleaned by a factory return value (`AX_HOOKS_ONLY_FOR_LIFECYCLE`, `AX_TIME_ENV_GLOBAL_LIFECYCLE`). The describe holds exactly ONE `let ctx`, not a pile of `let sut, let tick, let env` (`AX_NO_DESCRIBE_LET_PILEUP`).

Pattern.Intent: Preferred posture: SUT designed with injection seams is exercised against real `vi.fn()` stubs. No `vi.mock`, no operator confirm-gate triggered, no global patching — yet the test is fully isolated from the network.
Pattern.Why: No `vi.mock`, no `vi.spyOn`, no `globalThis.fetch` patching: SUT exposes injection seams (`httpRequest`, `store`) and the test passes typed `vi.fn()`s through them (`AX_MOCK_AS_LAST_RESORT`, `AX_HTTP_BOUNDARY_INJECTION`). Unified factory reused; case differs only by `response` override.

Anti_Pattern.Bad: Describe scope hosts `let sut, saveOrder, publish, order, now`; `beforeEach` assigns them; second case re-assigns shared `sut` to a different config (leaks state).
Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Pile of `let`-variables in describe scope (`AX_NO_DESCRIBE_LET_PILEUP`). Per-case data built in `beforeEach` without any teardown reason (`AX_HOOKS_ONLY_FOR_LIFECYCLE`, `AX_PREFER_FACTORY_OVER_HOOKS`). Second case re-assigns shared `sut`, leaking state across cases. No unified context type/factory (`AX_ONE_UNIFIED_CONTEXT_PER_FILE`).
Anti_Pattern.Good: ONE context type + ONE `createOrderLifecycleContext(overrides)` factory used by every case. Each case destructures the fields it uses; failure case overrides one field. No `beforeEach` (no teardown needed), no shared `let`s.

Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Three parallel factories for one SUT in one file (violates `AX_ONE_UNIFIED_CONTEXT_PER_FILE`). Each factory drifts independently; structural changes in `OrderLifecycle` require N edits, not one. Reader-agent must learn three shapes instead of one + a set of overrides; file size grows non-linearly with case count.
Anti_Pattern.Good: Single `createOrderLifecycleContext(overrides)` factory; happy/discount/failure cases pass different overrides through the same factory.

Anti_Pattern.Bad: `vi.mock('./http-client.ts', () => ({ httpClient: { get: vi.fn(...) } }))` for an in-process module; SUT instantiated as `new CatalogSync()` without injection.
Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: `vi.mock` introduced without operator confirm and without justification (`AX_MOCK_AS_LAST_RESORT`, `AX_MOCK_REQUIRES_OPERATOR_CONFIRM`). `httpClient` is in-process — the SUT could have accepted it as a constructor argument (`AX_HTTP_BOUNDARY_INJECTION`). Module mocks freeze a snapshot of `httpClient`'s shape; real changes there will not be caught.
Anti_Pattern.Good: SUT exposes injection seam: `class CatalogSync { constructor(deps: { httpRequest: HttpRequest }) {} }`. Test uses unified factory + `vi.fn()` stub through the seam (see `PT_DI_OVER_MOCK`).

Anti_Pattern.Bad: Agent runs `vitest --update` without operator review; uses `expect(renderModule(spec)).toMatchSnapshot()` with external `.snap` file for a small output.
Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Auto-update of stale snapshots with no operator visibility (`AX_SNAPSHOT_OPERATOR_CONFIRM`). External snapshot of a small output that should be inline (`AX_SNAPSHOT_USAGE_GATE`). Textbook fabrication path for green tests under agentic execution.
Anti_Pattern.Good: `expect(renderModule(spec)).toMatchInlineSnapshot('"/* generated module */\\nexport const value = 42;\\n"')`. For real changes the agent presents old vs new to operator and waits for confirmation.

Anti_Pattern.Bad: Test file has no imports (relies on `globals: true`). Inside a case: `vi.useFakeTimers(); vi.setSystemTime(...); vi.stubEnv('TICK', '1000');` without paired teardown. Next case in same file silently runs on fake timers.
Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Reliance on `globals: true` instead of explicit imports (`AX_EXPLICIT_VITEST_IMPORTS`). Fake timers and env stubs enabled without paired teardown (`AX_TIME_ENV_GLOBAL_LIFECYCLE`). State leaks into subsequent cases → order-dependent flicker.
Anti_Pattern.Good: Explicit `import { describe, it, expect, vi, beforeEach, afterEach } from 'vitest';`. `beforeEach` enables fake timers + env stubs; `afterEach` calls `vi.useRealTimers()` + `vi.unstubAllEnvs()`.

Anti_Pattern.Bad: `try { await client.reserveStock(''); expect.fail('should have thrown'); } catch (e) { expect((e as Error).message).toContain('idempotency'); }` AND inspecting `client._inflightCount` with `@ts-expect-error`.
Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Manual `try/catch + expect.fail` instead of `rejects.toThrow` (`AX_VITEST_ASSERT_API_CHOICE`). `toContain` on `error.message` (`AX_PARTIAL_STRING_VIA_MATCH`). Inspecting `_inflightCount` — protected internal field; `@ts-expect-error` bypasses encapsulation (inherited `AX_CONTRACT_OVER_IMPLEMENTATION`, `AX_ISOLATION_WITH_BEHAVIORAL_EVIDENCE`).
Anti_Pattern.Good: `await expect(client.reserveStock('')).rejects.toThrow(/idempotency.*empty|empty.*idempotency/i);`. For lifecycle observation: subscribe to `client.on('reservation.requested', e => events.push(e))` and assert `expect(events).toEqual([expect.objectContaining({ idempotencyKey: 'key-1' })])`.

Workflow_Outline.Step: Read contract + ticket's `Test Scenario Coverage`. Derive happy-path, boundary, failure scenarios. BDD scenarios in ticket are normative source of case names.

At top of test file define SINGLE `XxxContext` type and SINGLE factory `createXxxContext(overrides?)`. If teardown required → SINGLE lifecycle context object built in `beforeEach`/`afterEach`. Choose ONE preparation path for the whole file.
Estimate file size for cases planned. Projection > 300 LOC → plan a split now (by method or scenario family) before writing cases — extracting later more expensive.
For each `it`: trivial vs non-trivial. Non-trivial → opening brief and phase anchors. Pass stubs through injection seams; do NOT introduce `vi.mock` without operator confirm.
Start with simplest direct matcher. Aggregate into OBSERVE phase only when a single rich diff beats several isolated assertions.

Re-check: unified context maintained; no parallel factories; file under budget; anchors paired; snapshots not auto-updated; no ungated mocks; diagnostic quality intact. Run type-check + tests with coverage. Environment blocker → declared EXPLICITLY.

Hook.Expected: Exit 0; no `.only` / `.skip` / `.todo` without a deferred-ownership reference.
Exit 0; ≥1 happy + ≥1 boundary (when applicable) + ≥1 failure-path per public method. Environment blockers declared EXPLICITLY.

Hook.Purpose: Flag test files that exceed the 500 LOC hard ceiling or the 300 LOC soft target.
Hook.Expected: No test file exceeds 500 code-LOC. Files over 300 carry a tracked split task or explicit justification.

Hook.Purpose: Smoke-grep for forbidden patterns: `it.only`/`describe.only`/`it.skip`/`it.todo`, human narration `Step N`, `toContain` on `error.message`, manual `try/catch + expect.fail`.
Hook.Expected: Empty on new/changed test files (or matched lines carry explicit deferred-ownership reference).

Hook.Purpose: Every `vi.mock(` / `vi.hoisted(` MUST be paired with a justification comment within 3 lines (manual review).
Hook.Expected: Each match has an immediate-neighbor comment naming the dependency and the reason injection is unavailable.

Hook.Purpose: Verify the agent did NOT run snapshot updates autonomously.
Hook.Expected: Snapshot diffs presented to operator BEFORE commit; no `vitest --update` / `vitest -u` invocation in agent's command history.

Hook.Purpose: Smoke-grep for multiple `createXxxContext` definitions in the same file.
Hook.Command: rg --no-heading -nU "function create\w+Context" -t ts --glob '**/*.test.ts' | awk -F: '{c[$1]++} END {for (f in c) if (c[f] > 1) print c[f], f}'
Hook.Expected: No file declares more than one `createXxxContext` factory.

VitestRules.Reward_Criteria:
✅ Exactly ONE `XxxContext` type and ONE `createXxxContext` factory per file; all cases consume via overrides. New scenarios extend the existing factory rather than spawning parallel ones. ✅ Default preparation is the file's single factory called inside the case; describe-scope holds no mutable `let` populated by hooks. ✅ Hooks USED only for genuine teardown reasons; expose a SINGLE lifecycle context object — no `let`-pileup. ✅ Each describe uses ONE preparation path (factory-only OR lifecycle-context-only). ✅ Test files stay under 300 code-LOC (soft target) and never exceed 500 (hard ceiling); over-budget files split by method or scenario family with unified context preserved. ✅ Real modules + dependency injection are default; `vi.mock` / `vi.spyOn` / `vi.hoisted` absent unless paired with explicit justification AND operator confirmation. ✅ HTTP-touching code tested via injected ports stubbed with `vi.fn()`; existing project-level network-mocking harness reused consistently. ✅ Fake timers, `vi.stubEnv`, `vi.stubGlobal` paired with `vi.useRealTimers()` / `vi.unstubAll*()` (or runner config flags). ✅ Vitest APIs imported explicitly from `vitest`; default `isolate: true` preserved. ✅ Simplest direct matcher chosen (`toBe` / `toEqual` / `toStrictEqual` / `rejects.toThrow` / `toMatch`); error cases use `rejects.toThrow(SomeErrorClass)` + `toMatch` on stable message part. ✅ Snapshots only for stable serializable outputs too large for inline literals; preferred form is `toMatchInlineSnapshot`; ALL updates pass operator confirm-gate. ✅ Inherited rules (phase anchors, learning brief, BDD mapping, English-only, no human narration, contract coverage) honored.
❌ Multiple `createXxxContext` factories in one file, or ad-hoc inline construction alongside a factory. ❌ `describe`-scope hosts mutable `let` variables populated in `beforeEach`. ❌ `beforeEach` constructs values that have no teardown requirement. ❌ Test file exceeds 500 code-LOC, or exceeds 300 without a tracked split task. ❌ `vi.mock` / `vi.hoisted` without operator confirm-gate or without explicit justification. ❌ Mocking the SUT's own module, the co-tested collaborator, or the very contract method under test. ❌ Auto-updating snapshots (`vitest --update` / `vitest -u`) without operator review. ❌ Snapshot used for scalar or small object that would be clearer under `toBe` / `toEqual`. ❌ Reliance on `globals: true` instead of explicit imports from `vitest`. ❌ `toContain` on `error.message`, or manual `try/catch + expect.fail` instead of `rejects.toThrow`. ❌ Fake timers / env stubs / global stubs enabled without paired teardown. ❌ Test inspects protected/private/internal state of the SUT. ❌ Phase anchors missing on non-trivial case OR used around trivial one-line cases; anchor name restating syntax. ❌ Opening brief on trivial case OR missing brief on non-trivial case. ❌ BDD scenario from ticket has no corresponding `it` case OR mismatched canonical case name without ticket update. ❌ Tests written for `runtime-hook-required` / `e2e-required` scenarios at Vitest level without explicit deferred-ownership note. ❌ `console.*` in test, `Step N` narration, duplicate cases, non-English names/comments. ❌ Committed `it.only` / `describe.only` / `it.skip` / `it.todo` without deferred-ownership reference. ❌ Type-check / coverage / test run failed silently without EXPLICIT blocker statement.
